examples.title: Binary Addition Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to add two n-bit binary numbers with overflow detection
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Binary_Add(A[0..n-1], B[0..n-1])
BEGIN
  carry ← 0
  result[0..n-1] ← 0
  
  FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 DO
    sum ← A[i] + B[i] + carry
    result[i] ← sum MOD 2
    carry ← sum DIV 2
  END FOR
  
  overflow ← carry  // For unsigned, carry indicates result > 2^n
  
  // For signed overflow detection:
  // overflow = (A[n-1] == B[n-1]) AND (result[n-1] != A[n-1])
  
  RETURN result, carry, overflow
END
examples.explanation: Processes bits from LSB to MSB, propagating carry. For unsigned, carry out indicates overflow. For signed, overflow occurs when adding two numbers with same sign gives different sign result.

examples.title: Decimal to 2's Complement Conversion
examples.description: Convert decimal to n-bit 2's complement representation
examples.explanation: For positive numbers, direct binary conversion. For negative: add 2^n to get unsigned value, then convert. Reverse by checking MSB and subtracting 2^n if negative.

examples.title: IEEE 754 Single Precision Encoder
examples.description: Encode a floating point number to IEEE 754 single precision format
examples.explanation: Uses union to reinterpret float's bits as unsigned int. Prints sign, exponent, and mantissa separated. For -5.75: sign=1, exponent=129 (10000001), mantissa stores .0111.

examples.title: Binary to Gray Code Conversion
examples.description: Convert binary number to Gray code (used in K-maps and encoders)
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Binary_To_Gray(binary[0..n-1])
BEGIN
  gray[0] ← binary[0]  // MSB remains same
  
  FOR i ← 1 TO n-1 DO
    gray[i] ← binary[i-1] XOR binary[i]
  END FOR
  
  RETURN gray
END

ALGORITHM Gray_To_Binary(gray[0..n-1])
BEGIN
  binary[0] ← gray[0]
  
  FOR i ← 1 TO n-1 DO
    binary[i] ← binary[i-1] XOR gray[i]
  END FOR
  
  RETURN binary
END

// Example: Binary 1100 → Gray 1010
// gray[0] = 1
// gray[1] = 1 XOR 1 = 0
// gray[2] = 1 XOR 0 = 1
// gray[3] = 0 XOR 0 = 0
// Result: 1010
examples.explanation: Gray code: MSB stays same, each subsequent bit is XOR of current and previous binary bit. Reverse uses running XOR. Ensures only one bit changes between consecutive values.
